proposals.title: Amortized Meta-Reasoning for Dynamically Constrained Inference in Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) provide tractable inference through structural constraints like completeness and decomposability, but these constraints can be overly restrictive, limiting model expressiveness and scalability. Relaxing these constraints for more powerful models makes exact inference intractable, while naive approximations can violate critical dependencies, leading to inaccurate or unreliable predictions. An adaptive method is needed to manage this trade-off intelligently.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-reasoning module, trained via amortized inference, can dynamically predict where structural constraints in an SPN can be safely approximated and where they must be enforced, enabling more scalable and expressive models while preserving accuracy on critical queries.
proposals.method: We will develop a recurrent meta-reasoner network that takes as input the current state of the SPN (e.g., node activations, query) and outputs a gating policy over the graph structure. This policy will decide, for each region of the SPN, whether to enforce exact constraints or allow a controlled, learned relaxation. The meta-reasoner will be trained end-to-end using a reinforcement learning objective that balances inference speed against a reward based on fidelity to a validation set of exact inference results, drawing on techniques from relevance-guided modeling and optimization over deep networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new framework for scalable, approximate inference in deep probabilistic models that maintains theoretical guarantees where necessary. It will extend the practical applicability of SPNs to larger, more complex domains like the amodal 3D reconstruction tasks referenced in recent work. The meta-reasoning paradigm could influence how structural constraints are managed in other graphical models.
proposals.novelty: While amortized inference and learned approximations exist, dynamically and adaptively managing the enforcement of core model constraints (completeness/decomposability) via a dedicated meta-reasoner is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond static relaxation or uniform approximation schemes.

proposals.title: Stable, Decentralized Inference via Overlapping Decompositions in Loosely-Coupled Probabilistic Graphical Models
proposals.motivation: Exact inference in large probabilistic graphical models (PGMs) is computationally intractable, while standard variational or loopy belief propagation approximations often fail to scale or guarantee stability for complex, interconnected systems. Many real-world domains, such as multi-agent robotics or large-scale sensor networks, exhibit weak, intermittent interactions that are poorly served by tightly-coupled global inference, creating a need for principled, approximate decentralized algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting concepts from decentralized control—specifically overlapping decompositions and stability under approximate coupling—we can develop a new class of approximate inference algorithms that maintain provably bounded error while achieving substantial computational gains in loosely-coupled PGMs.
proposals.method: We will formalize metrics for inter-cluster coupling strength in PGMs, drawing on information-theoretic measures like conditional mutual information. Building on this, we will design a hybrid message-passing framework where clusters perform local, exact inference (e.g., using Sum-Product Networks for efficiency) and exchange beliefs only when local changes exceed a threshold derived from the coupling metric. The algorithm's stability and error bounds will be analyzed using perturbation theory adapted from control systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded framework for scalable, decentralized probabilistic inference with bounded approximation guarantees. It will yield practical algorithms applicable to large-scale, weakly-interacting systems in robotics and sensor networks, advancing beyond current variational and sampling-based methods.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct translation of stability and overlapping decomposition principles from decentralized control theory to probabilistic inference, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously explored to develop *provably stable* approximate inference schemes.

proposals.title: Approximate Inference in Relaxed Sum-Product Networks: A Stability and Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) guarantee efficient exact inference through strict completeness and decomposability constraints, but these limit their representational flexibility and ability to model complex, overlapping feature interactions common in real-world data like robotic scenes. Pre-2026, there is a gap in principled methods for learning and performing inference in SPNs where these structural constraints are intentionally relaxed to capture richer dependencies, trading theoretical guarantees for practical modeling power.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally relaxing the completeness and decomposability constraints in SPNs and employing a stability-driven optimization framework, we can develop models that maintain tractable approximate inference while significantly improving their capacity to learn complex, amodal representations for tasks like 3D reconstruction and dynamics prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel SPN architecture where sub-SPNs at sum nodes are allowed to have overlapping scopes (relaxed decomposability) and children of sum nodes can have unaligned distributions (relaxed completeness). Inference will be formulated as an optimization problem, using gradient descent to find approximate marginals, inspired by the 'kernel machine' view of deep networks. We will incorporate a relevance-guided mechanism, similar to Domingos's prior work, to dynamically prune or adjust connections during learning to maintain stability and control the approximation error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new class of probabilistic deep models that bridge the gap between the tractability of SPNs and the flexibility of general graphical models. It will deliver new algorithms for approximate inference and learning in these relaxed SPNs, with empirical validation on robotic manipulation benchmarks requiring robust 3D scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on maintaining exact inference, we explicitly break the core constraints of SPNs and develop a dedicated, stable optimization framework for the resulting intractable model, linking it to modern deep learning theory through the gradient descent-kernel perspective.

proposals.title: Solution-Adaptive Hybrid SPNs: Integrating Intractable Modules via Variational Interface Learning
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) provide tractable inference but are limited by their requirement for all components to be tractable, restricting their expressive power. Conversely, deep probabilistic models offer high expressivity but often have intractable partition functions, making exact inference and structural learning difficult. A principled method to combine these paradigms would enable models that are both highly expressive and retain tractable inference where it is most critical, addressing a core limitation in probabilistic deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid SPN architecture, with a variational embedding layer acting as a learned interface between a tractable SPN backbone and embedded intractable modules, can be trained effectively to expand representational power while preserving the global benefits of tractable learning and inference.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid SPN where the network graph is partitioned into a tractable domain (standard SPN) and intractable domains (e.g., deep energy-based modules). Inspired by RANS-LES coupling in CFD, we replace a fixed boundary with a solution-adaptive variational interface. This interface, parameterized as an inference network, will learn an approximate posterior to pass sufficient statistics (e.g., means, variances) and error gradients between domains. The intractable modules will be trained using variational inference or contrastive divergence, with their objectives regularized by the likelihood of the global tractable SPN, enabling end-to-end optimization via backpropagation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel hybrid probabilistic architecture that breaks the tractability-efficiency trade-off of pure SPNs. It will provide a formal framework and training algorithm for integrating black-box, expressive modules into tractable graphical models, expanding their applicability to complex, high-dimensional domains like robotics and vision where partial guarantees are essential.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the adaptation of solution-adaptive interface concepts from computational physics to probabilistic deep learning, creating a dynamic, learned variational bridge between tractable and intractable probabilistic components, which is a departure from rigid, pre-defined model structures.

proposals.title: Expressive Hybrid Sum-Product Networks: Integrating Intractable Sub-Models via Variational Inference and Annealed Importance Sampling
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) are tractable probabilistic models, but their expressiveness is limited by the requirement that all sub-networks have tractable partition functions. This prevents SPNs from incorporating powerful, modern deep generative components like energy-based models or certain neural likelihoods, restricting their application in complex domains like robotics and scene understanding where such representations are valuable.
proposals.hypothesis: By using a variational inference framework to bound the log-partition functions of intractable sub-networks, and employing Annealed Importance Sampling (AIS) for accurate gradient estimation, we can stably train hybrid SPNs that maintain overall tractability for queries while significantly expanding their representational power.
proposals.method: We will architect a hybrid SPN where leaf distributions can be intractable energy-based models (EBMs) or implicit models. For each such leaf, we introduce a variational approximator (e.g., a Gaussian or a small tractable SPN) to provide a lower bound on its log-partition function via the Gibbs variational principle. The overall SPN likelihood and the variational bounds will be optimized jointly using stochastic gradient descent. Gradients for the intractable leaves will be estimated using AIS within the REINFORCE framework, with control variates to reduce variance. The network structure learning will leverage pre-2026 relevance-guided methods to decide where to place intractable modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of probabilistic deep models that combine the query efficiency of SPNs with the expressiveness of intractable sub-models, a theoretical framework for their stable training, and empirical validation on challenging 3D object and robotic manipulation datasets, demonstrating improved density estimation and sample quality.
proposals.novelty: While variational methods and SPNs exist separately, their integration to systematically 'black-box' intractable components into an SPN's tractable inference flow is novel, moving beyond the strict tractability dogma without abandoning its computational benefits for marginal and conditional queries.

proposals.title: Kernel-Smoothed Sum-Product Networks: A Particle-Based Framework for Continuous Density Estimation
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) are a promising tractable probabilistic architecture, but their application to continuous data has been hampered by the need for variable discretization, which introduces information loss, artifacts, and complicates gradient-based learning. This limitation restricts SPNs in modern machine learning contexts where continuous, high-dimensional data is prevalent, such as in robotics and reinforcement learning, as seen in Domingos's recent work on object dynamics. A direct, native method for continuous density estimation within the SPN framework is a critical open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: By replacing discrete leaf distributions with non-parametric kernel density estimators or parametric Gaussian mixture models, an SPN can directly and tractably model continuous variables, leading to more accurate density estimates and enabling efficient end-to-end gradient-based optimization of the full network.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel SPN architecture where leaf nodes are continuous density estimators (KDEs/GMMs). Inspired by Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics, we will formulate the sum and product operations to act on these smoothed densities, ensuring tractability by maintaining the SPN's decomposability and completeness properties. We will derive parameter learning algorithms using Expectation-Maximization and gradient descent, leveraging the kernel viewpoint of learning, and implement inference via efficient message passing over continuous leaves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully continuous SPN formulation, eliminating the need for discretization. It will extend the applicability of tractable probabilistic models to continuous domains like robotics and control, and offer a new theoretical connection between kernel methods and probabilistic deep learning architectures.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual transfer of particle-based smoothing from computational physics to the structure of probabilistic networks, creating a 'field-based' SPN. This moves beyond simple replacement of discrete nodes with Gaussians by providing a unified framework for non-parametric and mixture-based leaves within the tractable inference paradigm.

proposals.title: Tractable Generative Modeling of Continuous Data via Sum-Product Networks with Exact Ancestral Sampling
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models like VAEs and GANs lack exact, tractable likelihoods, complicating tasks like anomaly detection and controllable generation. While Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) provide exact inference, their application has been largely limited to discrete or pre-discretized data, introducing artifacts and information loss for continuous domains like audio, sensor streams, and financial time-series. A model that natively handles continuous data with tractable densities and exact sampling would bridge a critical gap in probabilistic deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SPNs can be constructed with continuous leaf distributions (e.g., Gaussian, Gamma, or mixture distributions) to form a deep, tractable probabilistic model for raw continuous data, and that this representation enables an exact and efficient ancestral sampling algorithm for generating high-fidelity, realistic samples.
proposals.method: We will develop a continuous SPN architecture where leaf nodes are tractable continuous distributions, and internal nodes are weighted sums and products. Structure learning will leverage pre-2026 methods like LearnSPN but with a relevance-guided clustering of continuous variables. For parameter learning, we will derive and implement Expectation-Maximization (EM) updates or direct gradient-based optimization of the log-likelihood. Crucially, we will design an ancestral sampling algorithm that exploits the network's probabilistic circuit semantics to perform exact sampling by recursively choosing children at sum nodes proportional to their weights and drawing samples from product and leaf nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel deep generative model that provides exact density estimation and exact, efficient sampling for high-dimensional continuous data. It will establish a new benchmark for likelihood-based generative modeling on continuous domains, with direct applications in generating realistic synthetic waveforms and time-series for data augmentation and simulation.
proposals.novelty: While SPNs for hybrid domains exist, a deep SPN designed specifically for native, high-dimensional continuous data with a derived exact ancestral sampling procedure is novel. This contrasts with prior work that often required discretization or focused solely on density estimation, not high-quality generation.

proposals.title: Sum-Product Networks with Continuous Leaves: A Non-Parametric Framework for Exact Inference on Raw Data
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable exact inference but have traditionally required discrete variables or parametric assumptions (e.g., Gaussians) for continuous data, limiting their expressiveness and forcing potentially lossy discretization. A direct, non-parametric formulation for continuous variables would enable SPNs to be applied natively to high-dimensional real-world sensor data (e.g., robotics, vision), preserving information and integrating seamlessly with deep feature learning. This addresses a core limitation in scaling tractable probabilistic models to modern AI problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by replacing discrete leaf distributions with non-parametric density estimators based on kernel methods or normalizing flows, and by ensuring the resulting SPN's structure permits closed-form integration, we can construct a model that performs exact inference directly on continuous variables without information loss from discretization.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel SPN architecture where leaf nodes are continuous density estimators, such as Kernel Density Estimators (KDEs) or simple normalizing flows. The key technical challenge is maintaining tractability of the partition function; we will enforce a structure where the product of univariate KDEs at a product node remains analytically integrable. We will derive learning rules using backpropagation and gradient descent, optimizing the SPN parameters and the bandwidth parameters of the KDEs simultaneously, ensuring the model scales to high-dimensional data via deep stacking of sum and product layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully non-parametric SPN framework for raw continuous data, eliminating the discretization bottleneck. It will expand the applicability of tractable probabilistic models to domains like robotic perception and control, where Domingos's prior work on object dynamics operates. The resulting model will serve as a new baseline for density estimation and structured prediction with exact inference guarantees.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses Gaussian leaves or discretization, our approach is novel in its direct use of adaptive, non-parametric leaves within the SPN framework to model arbitrary continuous densities, maintaining exact inference—a combination not achieved in pre-2026 literature. It bridges Domingos's insights on kernel machines with the structural efficiency of SPNs.

proposals.title: Robust Causal and Graphical Structure Discovery via Multi-Hypothesis Testing with Adaptive Regularization
proposals.motivation: Learning the structure of networks (e.g., causal graphs, neural architectures, relational models) from data is fundamental to AI, but standard methods are prone to discovering spurious, dataset-specific dependencies. This fragility undermines interpretability and generalization, especially in high-dimensional settings or when data comes from multiple, potentially biased, environments. A principled framework to distinguish stable structural relationships from noise is critically needed for reliable inference in fields like robotics and reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing structure discovery as a constrained, multi-hypothesis testing problem with adaptive regularization, we can systematically identify a set of plausible network structures where the most robust and simple graphs correspond to stable, generalizable relationships invariant across environments.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that iteratively proposes candidate graph structures (hypotheses) using a base learner (e.g., a gradient-based structure search or a constraint-based method). For each candidate, we will apply environment-specific adaptive regularization, inspired by techniques from invariant risk minimization, to penalize edges that vary across data partitions. The core innovation is a testing procedure that evaluates the stability and likelihood of each graph under resampling and adversarial environment splits, outputting a ranked Pareto frontier of structures trading off fit, simplicity, and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodology for robust structure discovery that moves beyond single-point estimates to quantified uncertainty over graphical models. It will yield practical algorithms for learning more reliable causal models and neural architectures, directly applicable to problems like robust world modeling in reinforcement learning and interpretable feature learning.
proposals.novelty: While hypothesis testing and regularization are known, their integration into a constrained multi-hypothesis framework for structure discovery, with explicit environment-based validation to rank graphs by robustness, is a novel synthesis. This approach directly addresses the 'spurious discovery' problem in a way that prior constraint-based or score-based methods do not.

proposals.title: Phase Retrieval for Graph Discovery: Inferring Network Topology from Dynamical Outputs via Crystallographic Principles
proposals.motivation: A fundamental challenge in network science and machine learning is the inference of latent structure from observed data, especially when the connectivity is unknown. Current methods often rely on strong architectural priors or are limited to specific model classes. This work addresses the core problem of learning the graph adjacency matrix directly from time-series or relational outcome patterns, a task critical for understanding complex systems in neuroscience, social networks, and biological interaction maps where the true topology is hidden.
proposals.hypothesis: The inverse problem of network topology discovery can be effectively solved by adapting iterative phase retrieval algorithms from X-ray crystallography, treating the network's dynamical outputs as a diffraction pattern and the adjacency matrix as the unknown crystal lattice to be reconstructed.
proposals.method: We will formulate network inference as a constrained optimization problem, drawing direct analogy to the crystallographic phase problem. The 'intensity data' will be derived from statistical properties of observed node dynamics (e.g., covariance, spectral features). We will adapt the Hybrid Input-Output (HIO) and Error Reduction algorithms, enforcing physical network constraints (sparsity, non-negativity, degree distributions) in the Fourier domain analog. The optimization will be performed using gradient-based methods, potentially integrated with deep learning frameworks to learn a mapping from pattern features to topology initialization, improving convergence.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, principled computational framework for blind network discovery, moving beyond template-based architectures. It will yield a new class of algorithms applicable to domains where only system outputs are observable, advancing fields like causal structure learning and dynamical system identification. The work will also create a formal bridge between crystallographic reconstruction techniques and inverse problems in network science.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious core is the direct conceptual and algorithmic mapping from crystallographic diffraction to network inference, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously explored. Unlike typical graph learning methods, this approach does not start from a predefined graph model or likelihood but from an abstract 'pattern fitting' paradigm with iterative Fourier constraints.

proposals.title: Structure Learning for Sum-Product Networks via Differentiable Architecture Search and Stability Optimization
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, deep probabilistic modeling, but their performance is critically dependent on a manually designed or data-agnostic network structure. This limits their applicability to complex domains like the amodal 3D scene understanding and object dynamics modeling referenced in recent work, where the latent relational structure is unknown. Automating structure discovery is essential for SPNs to become a truly general-purpose, scalable learning framework.
proposals.hypothesis: The structure of an SPN can be effectively learned from data by formulating it as a differentiable architecture search problem, where the learned connectivity directly optimizes a combination of data likelihood and a stability-based structural prior, leading to more accurate and data-efficient models than fixed-structure SPNs.
proposals.method: We will develop a continuous relaxation of the SPN's graph structure, parameterizing the existence and strength of edges between sum and product nodes. A bi-level optimization will be performed: an inner loop learns node weights via standard gradient descent, while an outer loop uses gradient-based architecture search (e.g., DARTS) to learn the connectivity parameters. The search will be guided by a novel loss term enforcing structural stability—inspired by recent work on stability in 3D reconstruction—which penalizes architectures sensitive to small data perturbations, promoting robust connectivity patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first method for fully differentiable structure learning in SPNs, moving beyond heuristic or greedy pre-training methods. It will provide a principled, scalable tool for discovering latent variable interactions in probabilistic data, directly advancing themes of learning network structure and modeling object dynamics. The resulting models are expected to show superior performance on high-dimensional, structured data tasks where the graphical model is unknown.
proposals.novelty: While neural architecture search exists for discriminative deep networks, its application to the directed acyclic graph structure of probabilistic SPNs is novel. The integration of a stability-based structural prior, connecting to insights from robust 3D modeling, provides a new inductive bias for discovering physically plausible or causally relevant dependencies, going beyond mere likelihood maximization.

proposals.title: Bayesian Inference-Time Data Augmentation for Robust Prediction with Missing Features
proposals.motivation: Standard machine learning models fail at inference time when presented with partially observed inputs, a common scenario in robotics, healthcare, and sensor networks. Current solutions rely on separate, often deterministic, imputation methods that ignore the uncertainty inherent in the missing data, leading to overconfident and potentially erroneous predictions. A native, principled solution that integrates uncertainty quantification directly into the inference process is critically needed for robust real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by treating missing features at inference time as latent variables within a hierarchical Bayesian model, we can perform joint inference over predictions and plausible feature values, yielding a predictive distribution that correctly accounts for the uncertainty introduced by missingness, thereby improving robustness and calibration.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Bayesian data augmentation framework from Ecology to deep learning. The core architecture will be a Sum-Product Network (SPN), chosen for its tractable probabilistic inference, acting as the core predictive process P(Y | X). We will couple this with an observation model P(X_obs | X) for the missingness mechanism. At inference for a partially observed sample, we will use variational inference to approximate the posterior over the latent missing features, conditioning on the observed features and the SPN's parameters. This will enable efficient sampling from the joint predictive distribution P(Y | X_obs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel inference-time algorithm for handling missing data without pre-processing, directly outputting calibrated predictive distributions. It will advance the theory of robust inference under uncertainty and offer a practical tool for domains like robotics (e.g., for the amodal 3D reconstruction and object dynamics modeling problems Domingos has studied) where sensor data is often incomplete.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct translation of a Bayesian data augmentation scheme from a non-standard field (Ecology) into a native inference-time procedure for deep/structured probabilistic models, moving beyond static imputation to dynamic, prediction-aware uncertainty quantification during inference.

proposals.title: Stochastic Sum-Product Networks for Robust Inference with Arbitrary Missing Data Patterns
proposals.motivation: Real-world systems, from robotics to healthcare, must perform inference with partially observed inputs. Pre-2026 deep models often fail or require ad-hoc imputation, which introduces bias and computational overhead. A principled, native solution for handling missing data during inference is critical for deploying robust AI in uncontrolled environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a latent variable model directly into the structure of a Sum-Product Network (SPN), we can create a probabilistic graphical model that natively supports exact, efficient marginalization over any subset of missing input features during inference, without retraining or imputation.
proposals.method: We will develop Stochastic SPNs (S-SPNs), extending Domingos's SPN framework by treating input units as stochastic leaf distributions (e.g., Gaussian or Categorical) rather than deterministic indicators. During inference, missing variables are treated as latent and marginalized out via the SPN's inherent bottom-up sum-product pass. We will derive a variational EM learning algorithm that optimizes network parameters and the distributions over missing inputs, ensuring stability. The approach will be evaluated on robotics manipulation datasets (with occluded object properties) and standard UCI datasets with artificially masked features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded, tractable model for robust inference under missing data, advancing beyond simple imputation. It will extend the utility of SPNs to real-time applications like robotics where perception is incomplete. The method will also offer new insights into the relationship between deep network architectures and kernel methods under data uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While SPNs efficiently handle missing data by design, prior work assumes missingness is known at training time. Our method uniquely models inputs as latent variables, enabling the network to infer and marginalize over data missing arbitrarily *only* at test time, a capability not native to standard or deep SPNs.

proposals.title: PhyloSPN: Joint Structure and Parameter Learning for Sum-Product Networks via EM and Greedy Search
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer a deep, tractable probabilistic architecture, but their adoption is hindered by the lack of robust, automated learning algorithms for both structure and parameters. Current methods often rely on pre-defined structures or simple clustering, limiting their expressiveness and applicability to complex, high-dimensional data. This project addresses this core bottleneck in probabilistic deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: The joint learning problem for SPNs can be effectively solved by an iterative algorithm that alternates between Expectation-Maximization for parameter optimization and a likelihood-based greedy search for structure refinement, analogous to successful strategies in phylogenetic tree reconstruction.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that initializes a dense, complete SPN structure over a variable set. The E-step will compute sufficient statistics for all nodes; the M-step will update leaf distribution parameters and sum node weights. A structure search step will then greedily prune or merge sum nodes with low weight variance and recursively partition variables and data where likelihood gain is highest, guided by pairwise mutual information matrices—mirroring distance matrices in phylogenetics. This EM-search loop iterates until convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified algorithm for joint SPN learning directly from data, eliminating the need for hand-crafted structures. It will advance the field of tractable probabilistic models by enabling automated discovery of deep, interpretable hierarchies, with applications in density estimation and missing data imputation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal adaptation of the proven phylogenetic learning paradigm—integrating continuous parameter optimization with discrete topology search—to the domain of deep probabilistic networks, a connection not previously explored for SPNs.

proposals.title: Structure-Aware, Gradient-Based Optimization for Deep Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, deep probabilistic models with clear semantics, but their development has been hindered by a lack of robust, scalable learning algorithms. Existing methods often rely on hard clustering for structure learning or use expectation-maximization, which can be brittle and scale poorly with model depth and data dimensionality, limiting SPNs' application to complex real-world domains like robotics and scene understanding where uncertainty is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating SPN parameter learning as a constrained optimization problem directly amenable to gradient descent, and by integrating a data-driven, relevance-guided strategy for latent structure refinement, we can develop a more stable and scalable learning framework that unlocks the representational power of deep SPNs.
proposals.method: We will develop a batch gradient descent algorithm for SPNs by applying automatic differentiation to the network polynomial, ensuring computational tractability via the existing SPN DAG structure. To stabilize learning and improve structure, we will incorporate a regularization term inspired by stability and connectivity principles from amodal 3D reconstruction, penalizing representations that are sensitive to small input variations. Furthermore, we will guide the iterative refinement of sum node scopes (latent variable structure) using a relevance metric derived from feature importance in predicting object dynamics, creating a feedback loop between parameter optimization and structure adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully gradient-based, structure-aware learning algorithm for deep SPNs, enabling their training on high-dimensional data with modern optimizers. It will bridge the gap between SPNs' theoretical promise and their practical application, offering a new tool for probabilistic deep learning that is both interpretable and trainable at scale.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the seamless integration of gradient-based optimization with dynamic, relevance-guided structure learning within the SPN framework, moving beyond the traditional separation of these phases and leveraging insights from stability and predictive relevance in other domains.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Sum-Product Networks for Scalable, Interpretable Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Deep learning dominates large-scale vision but lacks the inherent uncertainty quantification and exact inference of probabilistic graphical models. Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer these advantages but have struggled to scale to billion-pixel datasets like ImageNet due to their monolithic structure and training complexity. This creates a critical gap between the scale of deep learning and the rigor of probabilistic models.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical SPN architecture, inspired by scalable hierarchical Bayesian models in computational ecology, can decompose large-scale vision tasks into coordinated, domain-specific sub-models, enabling tractable exact inference and effective learning on massive, heterogeneous image data.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-level SPN where leaf distributions model local image patches (e.g., via pre-trained CNN features). Mid-level SPNs, structured according to visual domains or object parts, will combine these patches. A top-level SPN, governed by a shared hierarchical prior over its parameters, will coordinate the mid-level models. Training will use a block-coordinate variant of the Expectation-Maximization algorithm, allowing parallel optimization of sub-SPNs. Inference will remain exact via the SPN's tree structure, propagating probabilities bottom-up.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable probabilistic architecture for vision that maintains exact inference. It will provide a principled framework for incorporating heterogeneous data sources and prior knowledge. The result bridges the scale of deep networks with the interpretability and uncertainty measures of SPNs.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is adapting the parameter hierarchy and spatial decomposition principles from large-scale ecological Bayesian networks to the structure and learning of SPNs, a previously unexplored cross-disciplinary analogy for achieving scalability.

proposals.title: AdaSPN: Adaptive, Boosting-Constructed Sum-Product Networks for Scalable and Calibrated Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Deep neural networks dominate large-scale vision but are often data-hungry, poorly calibrated, and lack tractable probabilistic semantics. Conversely, Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer exact, efficient inference but struggle with scalability and adaptive capacity. A synthesis is needed to build deep, probabilistically rigorous models that are data-efficient and robust, particularly for complex tasks like fine-grained recognition and scene understanding where uncertainty matters.
proposals.hypothesis: An SPN architecture constructed incrementally via a boosting paradigm will yield a model that combines the adaptive error-correction and robustness of boosting with the tractable probabilistic inference of SPNs, leading to superior data efficiency and calibration on large-scale vision tasks compared to monolithic SPNs or standard deep networks.
proposals.method: We will develop a boosting algorithm where each weak learner is a shallow SPN 'stump' (e.g., a product of univariate distributions or a small mixture). Each boosting round will add a new layer or sub-network to a growing SPN structure, with the weights of training examples updated based on the current model's marginal likelihood or classification error. The algorithm will optimize a loss function (e.g., exponential loss) compatible with SPN likelihoods, ensuring the final deep SPN remains a valid, tractable probability model. We will integrate this with convolutional feature extractors for vision tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, probabilistically sound architecture for vision that is built data-efficiently. It will provide a new theoretical link between ensemble methods and deep probabilistic networks. Empirically, we expect to demonstrate state-of-the-art calibration and robustness on datasets like ImageNet and ADE20K, while maintaining competitive accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal integration of boosting as a *construction algorithm* for deep SPNs, moving beyond static architectures. This is distinct from simply ensembling pre-trained SPNs or using boosting for model selection.

proposals.title: Deep Sum-Product Networks for Scalable and Interpretable Image Recognition
proposals.motivation: Deep neural networks dominate large-scale vision tasks but are often opaque and data-hungry, requiring vast labeled datasets. Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer a compelling alternative with inherent interpretability, exact and efficient inference, and the ability to learn from less data, but they have not been successfully scaled to complex, high-dimensional image datasets like ImageNet.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating deep convolutional feature extractors with structured, high-capacity SPNs as probabilistic classifiers, we can achieve competitive accuracy on large-scale vision benchmarks while providing a tractable probabilistic model that quantifies uncertainty and offers insights into its decision-making process.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid architecture where a standard convolutional neural network (e.g., ResNet) acts as a deep feature extractor. Its penultimate layer outputs will feed into a large, learnable SPN structured to reflect spatial and semantic hierarchies. We will employ online algorithms for SPN weight optimization, such as hard or soft gradient-based EM, and investigate parameter tying and region graph structures to manage the model's scale. The training will leverage standard backpropagation for the CNN component, coupled with specialized updates for the SPN to perform joint end-to-end learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first SPN-based model capable of competing with deep CNNs on datasets like ImageNet, establishing SPNs as a viable, scalable architecture for vision. It will provide a new class of vision models that natively output calibrated probabilities and whose internal structure can be inspected to understand feature combinations and failure modes.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the seamless, end-to-end integration of deep representation learning with the probabilistic semantics and exact inference of SPNs at a scale previously unattempted, moving beyond small, synthetic datasets to real-world vision challenges.

proposals.title: Automated Complexity Frontier Mapping for Partition Functions via Interactive Theorem Proving and Algorithm Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The partition function is a fundamental quantity in machine learning, underpinning probabilistic graphical models, Markov random fields, and Sum-Product Networks (SPNs). Pre-2026, its computational complexity is understood only through scattered, manually proven theorems for specific graph classes, creating a fragmented landscape. A systematic, automated map of the tractability frontier is needed to guide model design and algorithm selection in learning and inference.
proposals.hypothesis: The boundary between tractable (#P-easy) and intractable (#P-hard) classes of partition functions can be formally and automatically delineated by encoding known complexity-theoretic proofs into an interactive theorem prover and using algorithm synthesis to generate constructive witnesses for polynomial-time computability.
proposals.method: We will define a formal meta-language to specify partition function families via hypergraph structural parameters (e.g., treewidth, hypertree width) and function algebraic properties (e.g., type of potential, allowed operations). Using the Isabelle/HOL or Lean theorem prover, we will encode known reduction templates for #P-hardness and polynomial-time algorithms (e.g., variable elimination, junction tree). An automated reasoning engine will then analyze a given specification, attempt to construct a proof of tractability by synthesizing a concrete algorithm, or else fall back to constructing a gadget reduction to a known hard problem, verified by the prover.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dynamically generated, machine-verified complexity phase diagram for partition functions, providing a definitive reference for researchers. It will unify and extend pre-2026 complexity results, potentially discovering new tractable classes through automated exploration of the parameter space.
proposals.novelty: While complexity classifications exist, automating the proof process for entire families of problems using interactive theorem provers and algorithm synthesis is a novel synthesis of formal methods and computational complexity theory, moving beyond cataloging results to creating a generative proof system.

proposals.title: Phase Diagrams for Inference: Mapping Complexity Boundaries in Sum-Product Networks via Statistical Physics Analogies
proposals.motivation: The partition function is central to inference in probabilistic graphical models, but its exact computation is often intractable (#P-hard). While specific tractable families like Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) exist, a unified, predictive theory for when a model's partition function becomes tractable is lacking. Drawing an analogy from statistical physics, where phase diagrams predict macroscopic behavior from microscopic parameters, could provide a principled framework for understanding and engineering tractable models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sharp boundaries in the parameter space of graphical models (e.g., interaction strength, graph treewidth, or SPN depth/connectivity) separate regimes of polynomial-time computability from #P-hardness, and that these boundaries can be characterized by complexity-theoretic order parameters analogous to those in physical phase transitions.
proposals.method: We will formalize 'order parameters' such as the average interaction-to-treewidth ratio and spectral properties of weight matrices in deep SPNs. Using methods from parameterized complexity and average-case analysis, we will analytically derive phase boundaries for canonical models. We will then develop a diagnostic algorithm that, given a model specification, estimates its position relative to these boundaries using graph metrics and tensor network contractions, predicting its likely tractability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel 'complexity phase diagram' framework for probabilistic inference, offering theoretical guarantees for new subclasses of tractable models. It will also yield practical diagnostic tools for model designers, allowing them to structure networks and choose parameters to avoid intractable regimes, thus advancing the design of scalable, interpretable probabilistic AI.
proposals.novelty: While phase transitions in SAT and random graphs are studied, applying this lens to map the tractability of partition functions across a continuous model space is novel. It uniquely bridges statistical physics intuition with rigorous parameterized complexity to create a predictive, rather than merely descriptive, theory of inference.

proposals.title: Tractable Partition Functions via Stability and Composition in Deep Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: The partition function is central to probabilistic inference and learning in undirected graphical models and energy-based models, yet its exact computation is generally intractable. This intractability severely limits the application of rich probabilistic models in deep learning and robotics, where efficient marginalization is crucial for planning and uncertainty quantification. Recent work on Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) shows that specific structural decompositions guarantee tractability, but a general theory linking model architecture, parameterization, and optimization dynamics to partition function tractability is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the tractability of partition functions in deep, learned models is governed by a combination of architectural stability conditions (inspired by stability analysis in dynamical systems) and the compositional structure of the model's computational graph, which can be identified and enforced during optimization.
proposals.method: We will analyze the conditions for tractable partition functions by extending the theoretical framework of SPNs to broader deep network architectures. The approach integrates concepts from Domingos's recent work on stability in 3D reconstruction and kernel analysis of gradient descent. Specifically, we will: 1) Formalize 'relevance-guided' connectivity constraints in the factor graph of a model to ensure decomposability; 2) Use optimization paths from gradient descent to characterize when learned weights approximately induce a kernel-like structure with a closed-form partition function; 3) Develop a practical algorithm that regularizes deep energy-based models or variational autoencoders toward these tractability conditions, using spectral constraints on weight matrices to ensure stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel set of sufficient architectural and optimization-based conditions for tractable partition functions in learned models. It will yield a practical methodology for designing deep probabilistic models that retain expressive power while enabling exact inference, directly benefiting applications in robotic manipulation and reinforcement learning that require robust probabilistic reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing insights from stability analysis, kernel theory of deep learning, and SPN tractability into a unified framework, moving beyond known, restrictive SPN structures to conditions applicable to general deep networks optimized via gradient descent.

proposals.title: The SPN Benchmark Suite: A Rigorous Framework for Probabilistic Architecture Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, deep probabilistic inference, but their empirical standing remains unclear due to ad-hoc, non-standardized comparisons with other deep models like Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Deep Belief Networks (DBNs). This lack of a systematic evaluation framework hinders progress in probabilistic deep learning by obscuring the true strengths, weaknesses, and appropriate application domains for each architecture. A principled benchmark, analogous to those in computational chemistry or computer vision, is urgently needed to guide both theoretical development and practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, multi-dimensional benchmarking framework will reveal distinct, task-dependent performance profiles for SPNs compared to other deep probabilistic models, precisely delineating their niche in terms of inference speed, exact marginal computation, and data efficiency.
proposals.method: We will adapt the methodology of force field comparison in computational chemistry to machine learning. First, we will define a canonical suite of core probabilistic tasks: (1) density estimation (log-likelihood), (2) marginal inference, (3) conditional query answering (MPE), and (4) missing data imputation. For each task, we will select standardized, pre-2026 datasets of varying complexity (e.g., MNIST, 20 Newsgroups, synthetic graphical model data). We will then implement controlled evaluation protocols, fixing computational budgets (FLOPs, memory) and hyperparameter tuning regimes, to fairly compare SPNs against benchmarked implementations of VAEs, DBNs, and standard deep neural networks with probabilistic outputs. Performance will be measured using a vector of metrics including accuracy, calibration, and inference latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, reproducible benchmark suite for deep probabilistic architectures, enabling direct and fair comparison. It will yield a clear, empirical map of the trade-offs between different model families, guiding researchers in model selection and identifying critical frontiers for SPN improvement. The framework itself will become a community standard for evaluating new probabilistic models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, chemistry-inspired benchmarking approach applied to probabilistic deep learning, moving beyond point comparisons to a holistic evaluation under controlled constraints. This formalizes architectural comparison into a rigorous scientific discipline for the field.

proposals.title: The Sum-Product Network as a Deep Learner: A Unified Framework for Comparing Tractability, Expressivity, and Optimization
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer a unique deep architecture with exact, tractable inference, a significant advantage over other deep models like Deep Belief Networks or Variational Autoencoders that require approximate inference. However, their empirical performance and theoretical properties relative to mainstream deep learning architectures remain underexplored, limiting their adoption. A systematic comparison is needed to clarify when SPNs' tractability trade-offs are beneficial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SPNs, when optimized with modern gradient-based techniques, can achieve competitive performance on structured data tasks (e.g., image completion, density estimation) compared to deep generative models, while providing superior calibration and uncertainty quantification due to their exact probabilistic semantics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study comparing SPNs against benchmark architectures (VAEs, Normalizing Flows, and Deep Kernel Machines) on curated datasets (e.g., dynamically binarized MNIST, multi-object scenes). Theoretically, we will analyze the circuits using the framework of 'inductive biases' and 'computational graphs,' comparing the effective kernel of a gradient-trained SPN to that of a deep neural network as outlined in Domingos' recent kernel machine analysis. Optimization will use the standard backpropagation-through-SPN method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, direct performance benchmark of SPNs against contemporary deep models, establishing clear strengths and weaknesses. It will yield a novel theoretical lens connecting SPN circuit structure to the implicit regularization of gradient descent, bridging probabilistic graphical models and deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dual empirical-theoretical approach, specifically applying the 'deep models as kernel machines' perspective to SPNs to explain their learning dynamics, and a rigorous evaluation focused on the interplay between tractable inference and representational power.

proposals.title: Message-Passing Structure Learning for Sum-Product Networks via Analogies to LDPC Decoding
proposals.motivation: Learning the structure of Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) remains a significant challenge, often requiring expensive search or restrictive assumptions. Current methods, like LearnSPN, use greedy, top-down recursive partitioning, which can lead to suboptimal structures and does not easily incorporate global consistency constraints. A principled, unified framework for joint structure and parameter learning is needed to improve the efficiency and accuracy of SPNs for high-dimensional, relational data.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing SPN structure learning as a decoding problem on a factor graph of potential decompositions and subsets, a message-passing algorithm analogous to the Sum-Product Algorithm for LDPC codes can efficiently and jointly infer a globally consistent, near-optimal hierarchical model.
proposals.method: We will construct a factor graph where variable nodes represent candidate subsets of random variables and factor nodes encode constraints (e.g., mutual exclusivity of partitions, completeness of covers) and local likelihoods from data. Inspired by LDPC decoding, we will derive message update rules that propagate beliefs about the validity of decompositions and variable clusterings. This iterative, belief-propagation-based procedure will replace greedy recursion, allowing soft decisions to be revised based on global information, ultimately converging on a structure and its parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified algorithm for SPN learning that breaks the local-optima limitations of greedy methods. It will advance the theory of probabilistic graphical model learning by establishing a formal bridge to error-correcting code theory. The resulting models are expected to show improved accuracy and data efficiency in density estimation tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual and technical transfer of iterative decoding from communications engineering to the open problem of probabilistic circuit structure learning, a connection not previously explored. This moves beyond standard gradient-based or combinatorial optimization to a constraint-satisfaction perspective.

proposals.title: Efficient Parameter Learning for Sum-Product Networks via Structured Belief Propagation
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable inference but often rely on Expectation-Maximization (EM) or gradient descent for learning, which can be slow and prone to local optima. Belief propagation (BP) in graphical models provides a powerful, distributed framework for exact or approximate inference and parameter estimation. Applying BP to SPNs could address these optimization challenges, leading to faster, more stable learning, which is critical for scaling SPNs to complex, high-dimensional data in robotics and vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating the SPN learning problem as a message-passing inference task on a suitably constructed factor graph will yield more efficient and globally consistent parameter estimates compared to standard gradient-based or EM approaches.
proposals.method: We will construct a factor graph representation of the SPN where sum, product, and variable nodes correspond to factors. We will derive message-passing rules (sum-product and max-sum algorithms) for both upward and downward passes to compute marginals and perform Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). This involves adapting BP to handle the hierarchical mixture structure of SPNs and integrating it with hard-EM for latent variable states. The algorithm will be implemented and tested on standard density estimation benchmarks and structured vision datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled algorithm for SPN parameter learning with potential convergence and efficiency benefits. It will deepen the theoretical connection between SPNs and probabilistic graphical models. Successful application would make SPNs more competitive for large-scale probabilistic modeling tasks in domains like the amodal 3D reconstruction and object dynamics modeling referenced in recent work.
proposals.novelty: While BP is standard for inference in graphical models, its direct application to the *parameter learning* problem in SPNs, by treating parameters as random variables in a factor graph, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis. This differs from using BP merely for inference within an existing SPN.

proposals.title: Derivative-Free Optimization of Sum-Product Networks via Natural Evolution Strategies
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer a tractable deep probabilistic architecture, but their training often relies on Expectation-Maximization or gradient-based methods derived from the specific circuit structure. This can limit flexibility, complicate integration with non-differentiable components, and pose challenges for novel SPN architectures where deriving exact gradients is non-trivial. A robust, gradient-free optimization method would enhance the applicability of SPNs in hybrid systems and black-box learning scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Natural Evolution Strategies (NES), a black-box optimization technique, can effectively train SPNs by estimating gradients of the data log-likelihood through parameter sampling, achieving comparable or superior performance to standard methods, especially in settings with non-differentiable operations or complex parameter constraints.
proposals.method: We will adapt the NES algorithm to optimize the weights (sum node parameters) and potentially the structure of an SPN. The core method treats the SPN's parameter vector θ as a search distribution, samples perturbations ε, and evaluates the log-likelihood on a batch of data for each perturbed model. The gradient of the expected log-likelihood with respect to θ is then estimated from these samples, enabling gradient ascent without backpropagation through the network's probabilistic graph. We will benchmark this against standard SPN training (e.g., hard/soft EM, SGD) on standard density estimation datasets, analyzing convergence and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, flexible training paradigm for SPNs, decoupling their probabilistic representation from gradient-based learning constraints. It will expand the toolkit for probabilistic deep learning, enabling the use of SPNs in broader, potentially non-differentiable pipelines. The research will also yield empirical insights into the optimization landscapes of deep probabilistic models.
proposals.novelty: While NES is established in reinforcement learning and evolutionary robotics, its application to training deep probabilistic graphical models like SPNs is unexplored. The novelty lies in bridging these fields to create a derivative-free, sampling-based optimizer specifically for SPNs, moving beyond their traditional training frameworks.

proposals.title: End-to-End Gradient-Based Learning for Deep Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, interpretable probabilistic models, but their training has largely relied on hard EM algorithms or closed-form updates, limiting their integration into deep, end-to-end learning pipelines. This restricts their application in complex domains like robotics and reinforcement learning, where gradient-based optimization of deep networks has proven dominant. Bridging this methodological gap is crucial for unlocking the potential of SPNs as robust, probabilistic components within modern deep learning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That deep SPNs can be effectively trained via backpropagation and stochastic gradient descent (SGD), leading to competitive or superior performance on density estimation and structured prediction tasks compared to traditional SPN learning methods, while enabling seamless composition with deep neural network modules.
proposals.method: We will design a library of differentiable, parameterized SPN layers (sum, product, leaf distributions) and stack them into deep architectures. The key technical challenge is ensuring numerical stability during gradient flow through the network's high-degree polynomials. We will adapt techniques from deep learning, such as gradient clipping, careful initialization, and the use of soft-plus activations for sum weights, and employ automatic differentiation (e.g., PyTorch/TensorFlow) for end-to-end training with SGD or Adam. We will benchmark against classical SPN learners on standard density estimation datasets (e.g., MNIST, BSDS300) and explore hybrid models where SPNs are trained jointly with neural feature extractors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework for gradient-based optimization of deep SPNs, enabling their use as plug-and-play probabilistic layers. It will expand the applicability of SPNs to tasks requiring joint optimization with deep perception models, such as the amodal 3D reconstruction and object dynamics modeling relevant to Domingos's recent research themes. The resulting models will combine the representational power of deep learning with the exact probabilistic inference of SPNs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored gradient updates for SPN parameters, the end-to-end training of deep, structured SPNs using backpropagation—treating them analogously to deep neural networks—is a novel and non-obvious direction. It directly challenges the traditional algorithmic separation between probabilistic graphical models and deep learning optimization.

proposals.title: Structured Sparsity for Robust Sum-Product Networks: An Elastic Net and Group Lasso Regularization Framework
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) are tractable deep probabilistic models, but their structure learning and parameter estimation can overfit, especially with limited data. Overfitting leads to overly complex networks that generalize poorly, a problem analogous to high-dimensional regression in genomics where thousands of correlated predictors exist. Pre-2026, SPN regularization has largely relied on simple L1/L2 penalties, lacking mechanisms for structured sparsity that prune entire model components.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Elastic Net and Group Lasso regularization, adapted from genomics, to SPN learning will induce structured sparsity in sum node weights, effectively pruning branches and selecting simpler mixture components, thereby yielding sparser, more robust networks with better generalization.
proposals.method: We will develop a new objective function for SPN parameter learning that combines: 1) a Group Lasso penalty on the weight vectors of each sum node, treating the weights to its children as a group to encourage pruning of entire branches, 2) a traditional L2 ridge penalty on all network parameters for magnitude control, and 3) the standard data log-likelihood. This hybrid 'SPN Elastic Net' objective will be optimized via stochastic gradient descent with proximal operators for the non-smooth Group Lasso term. We will integrate this into structure learning algorithms like LearnSPN, applying regularization during both the hierarchical clustering and parameter optimization phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled regularization framework for SPNs that directly controls graph complexity, mitigating overfitting. It will deliver new algorithms for learning more interpretable and data-efficient SPNs. The successful cross-pollination from genomics demonstrates a valuable methodology transfer between disparate fields of machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While Elastic Net is known, its combination with Group Lasso for structured sparsity in SPNs is novel. Applying group-level penalties to sum nodes to perform implicit structure simplification during parameter learning is a non-obvious and previously unexplored approach for this model class.

proposals.title: Structured Regularization for Sum-Product Networks: Enhancing Generalization via Graph-Induced Priors
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) are tractable deep probabilistic models, but their structure learning and parameter estimation can overfit, especially with limited data. While standard L1/L2 regularization on weights is common in deep learning, its principled application to SPNs—which have a unique layered, probabilistic-graph structure—remains underexplored. Preventing overfitting is crucial for deploying robust SPNs in domains like the robotic manipulation and model-based RL highlighted in Domingos's recent work, where reliable uncertainty is key.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating graph-aware structural regularization (e.g., Laplacian-based smoothness across similar sum nodes, or group sparsity on subtree weights) during SPN parameter learning will significantly improve generalization performance compared to standard weight decay, without compromising computational tractability.
proposals.method: We will formulate a regularized maximum likelihood objective for SPNs, combining data likelihood with novel penalty terms. For L1, we will apply group lasso to entire sub-SPN weight vectors to induce subtree sparsity. For structural regularization, we will design a graph Laplacian regularizer based on the SPN's DAG to enforce smoothness of parameters across nodes with similar scope and children. We will develop efficient stochastic gradient descent optimizers for these objectives, leveraging the existing differentiable SPN frameworks, and test on benchmark density estimation and latent variable modeling tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for regularization in SPNs, delivering more data-efficient and generalizable models. It will extend the statistical learning theory of regularization to the unique algebraic and graphical properties of SPNs. The resulting techniques will be directly applicable to improving the robustness of SPNs used for state estimation and dynamics modeling in robotics and reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: While regularization is standard in neural networks, its tailored application to SPNs must respect their probabilistic semantics and DAG structure, making direct translation non-trivial. The proposed graph-induced structural regularizers are novel for SPNs and move beyond simple weight decay to leverage the model's own architecture.

proposals.title: A Complexity-Theoretic Foundation for Sum-Product Networks via Isomorphic Embeddings into Arithmetic Circuits
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) are a promising tractable probabilistic architecture, but their formal representational power and inherent limitations are poorly understood compared to other deep models. A long-standing analogy links SPNs to arithmetic circuits, yet this has remained a superficial metaphor without rigorous, structure-preserving translations that could import concrete lower-bound results from circuit complexity theory. Establishing such a formal bridge is critical for a principled theory of SPN efficiency, guiding architecture design and understanding fundamental trade-offs in probabilistic modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, well-defined classes of SPNs (e.g., those with decomposable and complete scopes) are isomorphic to restricted classes of monotone, multilinear arithmetic circuits, and that known lower bounds on circuit size for explicit polynomial families directly translate into provable lower bounds on the size of SPNs required to represent corresponding families of probability distributions.
proposals.method: We will first construct explicit, injective mappings from SPNs satisfying specific structural constraints (decomposability, completeness) to polynomial-sized monotone multilinear arithmetic circuits, preserving the computed function. We will then analyze the inverse mapping, identifying which circuit classes have SPN counterparts. Leveraging pre-2026 lower-bound results (e.g., for permanent, determinant, or specific multilinear polynomials), we will translate these into minimal size/depth requirements for SPNs. The analysis will employ tools from algebraic complexity and will be validated by constructing explicit distribution families that are provably hard for sub-critical SPN architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous complexity-theoretic foundation for SPNs, moving from analogy to formal proof. It will yield concrete, provable limitations on SPN efficiency for representing certain distributions, informing the design of learning algorithms and network architectures. The results will establish a new lens through which to analyze tractable probabilistic models, connecting them to the rich literature of circuit complexity.
proposals.novelty: While the SPN-arithmetic circuit connection is often noted, constructing explicit isomorphisms and performing targeted complexity translation to derive concrete, non-asymptotic lower bounds for SPNs is a novel and non-trivial formalization. This moves beyond descriptive analogy to yield actionable, provable statements about representational capacity.

proposals.title: Computational Parsimony: A Phylogenetic Framework for Analyzing Complexity in Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) are a tractable deep architecture for probabilistic modeling, but theoretical tools for analyzing their structural complexity and expressive efficiency remain underdeveloped. This limits our ability to compare architectures, prove lower bounds, and understand the trade-offs inherent in SPN design. Drawing an analogy from computational biology, where phylogenetic tree analysis rigorously scores evolutionary hypotheses, offers a novel lens for this analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SPN structures can be scored by a 'computational parsimony' measure, analogous to phylogenetic parsimony, which quantifies the minimal essential computational operations required to represent a distribution. This measure will provide a formal basis for establishing lower bounds on SPN size and for comparing the representational efficiency of different SPN architectures.
proposals.method: We will formalize the SPN as a directed acyclic computational phylogeny. We will define a set of elementary 'mutations' (e.g., insertion/deletion of sum nodes with specific weights, factorization via product nodes) and develop an algorithm, inspired by the Fitch-Hartigan algorithm for phylogenetic parsimony, to compute the minimal number of such operations required to transform a canonical, maximally expanded SPN into a given target SPN. This score will be computed for SPNs learned via standard methods (e.g., LearnSPN) and for families of known distributions to establish complexity bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, theoretically grounded complexity measure for SPNs, enabling direct comparison of architectural efficiency. It will provide a framework for deriving lower bounds on SPN size for representing specific distributions, advancing the theoretical understanding of SPNs. The cross-disciplinary analogy will also open new methodological connections between machine learning and computational biology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and algorithmic transfer of phylogenetic tree scoring techniques (specifically maximum parsimony) to the analysis of probabilistic graphical models, a connection not previously explored in SPN literature. This provides a non-obvious, principled alternative to simple node-counting heuristics for complexity.

proposals.title: Circuit Complexity Bounds for Sum-Product Networks: A Formal Analysis of Expressive Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs), pioneered by Domingos and colleagues, offer a tractable deep probabilistic architecture, but a rigorous theoretical understanding of their expressive power and efficiency compared to other deep models remains incomplete. Pre-2026, the field lacks formal tools to characterize the function classes SPNs can compactly represent and the problems where they provably outperform standard deep networks, hindering principled architecture design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tools from arithmetic circuit complexity—specifically, analysis of polynomial representation size, depth reduction, and separation results—can formally establish the families of functions that SPNs can represent efficiently (with polynomial size) and those for which they require exponential size, thereby delineating their fundamental capabilities and limitations.
proposals.method: We will map SPN structures onto arithmetic circuits over the reals, treating sum nodes as linear combinations and product nodes as multiplications. Using this formalization, we will apply lower-bound techniques (e.g., partial derivatives, rank arguments) to prove minimum circuit size for specific function families relevant to machine learning (e.g., symmetric functions, certain piecewise polynomials). Concurrently, we will construct explicit polynomial-size SPNs for other families and analyze the complexity of transforming trained deep networks into approximately equivalent SPNs, leveraging insights from the 'kernel machine' view of gradient descent.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic circuit-complexity framework for SPNs, yielding concrete separation theorems that explain when SPNs are a compact representation. It will offer theoretical guidance for applying SPNs to problems like robust 3D reconstruction and object dynamics modeling, where their inherent probabilistic structure is advantageous, and suggest new, provably efficient SPN architectures.
proposals.novelty: While SPNs have been analyzed for tractability of inference, applying arithmetic circuit complexity tools to deeply analyze their representational *efficiency* and prove formal separations from other deep models is a novel theoretical direction not yet explored in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: BoostSPN: Incremental Structure Learning for Sum-Product Networks via Functional Gradient Boosting
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, deep probabilistic models, but their structure is typically learned in a single batch, which can be suboptimal and computationally intensive. This limits their scalability and adaptability to streaming data or complex, high-dimensional domains like the robotic manipulation and object dynamics modeling central to Domingos's recent work. A principled, incremental learning method for SPNs is needed to overcome these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying functional gradient boosting—a powerful ensemble technique—to incrementally construct the layers of an SPN will yield a model that is more accurate, data-efficient, and robust than SPNs learned via standard batch structure learning algorithms.
proposals.method: We will adapt the AnyBoost framework to SPNs, treating each new sum or product node as a weak learner that minimizes a probabilistic loss function (e.g., negative log-likelihood). The algorithm will start with a simple base SPN (e.g., a single leaf layer) and iteratively add layers by fitting weak learners to the functional gradient of the loss. We will specifically explore gradient-based strategies for selecting the scope and type (sum vs. product) of new nodes, ensuring the decomposability and completeness properties of SPNs are maintained throughout the boosting process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first boosting-based algorithm for incremental SPN structure learning, directly addressing scalability and optimization challenges. It will deliver a new class of probabilistic models that combine the representational power of deep SPNs with the adaptive, ensemble strengths of boosting, validated on benchmark density estimation and relevant robotic perception tasks.
proposals.novelty: While boosting is well-established for discriminative models and has been applied to simple probabilistic circuits, its use for incrementally constructing the deep, layered structure of a full SPN is a novel and non-trivial synthesis of ensemble learning and tractable probabilistic inference.

proposals.title: Adversarially Robust and Compact Sum-Product Networks for Secure Embedded Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of machine learning models on resource-constrained embedded devices for critical applications (e.g., autonomous systems, IoT) is growing, but these models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks and must operate under strict power and memory budgets. Current robust deep learning models are computationally expensive and ill-suited for embedded platforms, while existing compact models like SPNs lack explicit defenses against adversarial perturbations. This creates a critical gap for secure, reliable, and efficient edge intelligence.
