proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating adversarial robustness directly into the structure learning and parameter optimization of Sum-Product Networks, we can produce models that are simultaneously compact, energy-efficient, and certifiably robust to norm-bounded input perturbations.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel learning algorithm for SPNs that incorporates a tailored adversarial training procedure, using Projected Gradient Descent to generate perturbations during optimization to minimize a robust loss function. We will co-optimize for model size via structure sparsification and leaf distribution simplification. Furthermore, we will design lightweight runtime monitors based on the SPN's internal probability and node activation statistics to flag distributional shifts indicative of adversarial inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first framework for constructing adversarially robust SPNs with guaranteed compactness, enabling secure inference on embedded hardware. It will contribute new algorithms for robust probabilistic model learning and provide a benchmark for evaluating the robustness-efficiency trade-off in non-deep learning models for embedded systems.
proposals.novelty: The joint optimization of adversarial robustness and model compactness within the SPN framework is novel, as prior robustness research focuses almost exclusively on deep neural networks, and SPN research has prioritized scalability and interpretability over security.

proposals.title: Organelle Networks: Power-Aware, Modular Sum-Product Networks via Specialized Subnet Inference
proposals.motivation: Deploying probabilistic models like Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) on embedded and IoT devices is hindered by their computational and memory footprint, which conflicts with strict power and latency constraints. Current model compression and pruning techniques often degrade the model's expressive power or require costly retraining. A new architectural paradigm is needed to enable efficient, on-device probabilistic inference without sacrificing core functionality.
proposals.hypothesis: By decomposing a monolithic SPN into a collection of minimal, specialized, and hard-optimized 'subnet organelle' modules—each responsible for a frequent, distinct inference pattern—and employing a dynamic, query-aware scheduler, we can achieve drastic reductions in active power and memory use during inference while preserving model accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm to automatically decompose a trained SPN into a set of minimal, frequently-used subnetworks, treating each as a hardened, fixed-function computational module. Inspired by Domingos's 'Every Model... a Kernel Machine' framework, we will formalize these subnets as specialized kernels for core inference operations. A lightweight, power-aware runtime scheduler, informed by query structure, will activate only the necessary module(s), keeping others in a low-power dormant state. We will employ combinatorial optimization and sparsity-inducing techniques from 'Relevance-Guided Modeling' to identify the optimal set of organelle modules and their activation policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, biologically-inspired architecture for ultra-efficient SPNs, enabling their deployment in resource-constrained environments. It will provide a new methodology for creating modular, event-driven probabilistic models and a theoretical analysis linking modular SPN structure to kernel-based efficiency gains.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct architectural analogy to cellular compartmentalization for probabilistic networks and its focus on dynamic, query-driven subnetwork activation—a departure from static model compression or monolithic accelerator designs prevalent in pre-2026 embedded deep learning.

proposals.title: Decentralized Probabilistic Inference via Federated Sum-Product Networks on Embedded Swarms
proposals.motivation: Emerging applications in pervasive sensing and robotic swarms require complex probabilistic reasoning in environments with severe energy, bandwidth, and latency constraints, where centralized cloud computation is infeasible. Current distributed machine learning paradigms, like federated learning, focus on training and require significant coordination, failing to provide lightweight, real-time collaborative inference for structured probabilistic models.
proposals.hypothesis: That a large Sum-Product Network (SPN) can be decomposed into specialized, miniaturized fragments distributed across a swarm of embedded devices, and that a decentralized message-passing protocol can orchestrate these fragments to perform globally consistent, approximate inference without a central coordinator.
proposals.method: We will develop a method to factor a pre-trained, large SPN into minimal, coherent sub-SPNs, each hosted on a separate device. A novel swarm inference protocol will be designed, extending the sum-product algorithm to operate over a peer-to-peer network. Devices will dynamically form transient clusters based on query relevance and network proximity, passing and aggregating partial marginals using a gossip-like communication scheme with confidence weights to ensure convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new systems-algorithms paradigm for federated inference, enabling complex probabilistic reasoning in decentralized, resource-constrained networks. It will provide a formal framework for the compositional distributed execution of SPNs and demonstrate its efficacy on a swarm robotics testbed for collaborative environment mapping.
proposals.novelty: While SPNs have been studied for efficient centralized inference and some distributed training exists, the fusion of model miniaturization, ad-hoc cluster formation, and a fully decentralized sum-product protocol for federated inference is a novel direction, moving beyond parameter averaging to the distribution of model structure and computation.

proposals.title: Structured Sparsity and Relevance-Guided Pruning for Ultra-Compact Sum-Product Networks on Microcontrollers
proposals.motivation: The deployment of probabilistic machine learning models on resource-constrained embedded devices (e.g., IoT sensors, wearables) is limited by the computational and memory footprint of existing models. While Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable inference, their standard implementations are too large for low-power microcontrollers with severe SRAM and flash memory constraints, hindering real-time, on-device probabilistic reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a novel, multi-stage compression pipeline combining relevance-guided structural sparsification—inspired by object-dynamics modeling—and aggressive parameter quantization, we can create ultra-compact SPNs that retain high probabilistic fidelity for key inference tasks on embedded targets.
proposals.method: We will develop a compression framework starting with pre-trained, task-specific SPNs. First, we will apply structured pruning guided by an edge-relevance metric derived from the network's weight sensitivity and functional impact on output distributions, akin to techniques in model-based RL. Second, we will employ a hardware-aware quantization scheme, mapping sum weights and leaf parameters to low-bit integer representations. Finally, we will co-optimize the pruned architecture and quantization levels using a straight-through estimator during fine-tuning to recover accuracy, targeting deployment via TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel methodology for creating miniaturized SPNs with sub-100KB memory footprints, 2) an open-source toolchain for SPN compression and deployment on ARM Cortex-M series MCUs, and 3) empirical benchmarks on embedded perception tasks demonstrating viable accuracy-power trade-offs previously unattainable with probabilistic models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic neural network pruning, our approach explicitly leverages the unique probabilistic semantics and tree-like structure of SPNs for sparsification, and integrates relevance guidance from dynamic modeling to preserve critical probabilistic dependencies, a direction unexplored in pre-2026 SPN compression literature.

proposals.title: Scalable, Distributed Inference for Sum-Product Networks via Tensor Network Decomposition and MapReduce
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable inference for high-treewidth models but scaling them to massive datasets and complex structures is limited by memory and compute on single machines. Pre-2026, large-scale probabilistic inference remains a core challenge in machine learning. Distributed computing paradigms like MapReduce have revolutionized other fields (e.g., computational chemistry for tensor contractions) but have not been systematically applied to the specific computational graph of SPNs.
proposals.hypothesis: The inference computation of an SPN can be formally decomposed into a tensor network, enabling its efficient parallelization across a distributed cluster using a MapReduce-style execution model without sacrificing exactness, thereby overcoming single-node memory and computational bottlenecks.
proposals.method: We will develop a compiler that takes a compiled SPN graph and partitions it into independent sub-networks (tensor slices) based on scope analysis. Using a MapReduce framework (e.g., Apache Spark), we will map the evaluation of each partition, along with its slice of input data, to worker nodes. A hierarchical reduction tree, inspired by communication-optimal tensor contraction algorithms, will aggregate partial sums and products, minimizing data shuffling. The core algorithm will extend the classic bottom-up SPN inference pass into a distributed schedule of tensor operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first distributed inference algorithm for SPNs that is exact and communication-aware, enabling the application of very large SPNs to big data problems. It will also contribute a novel formal connection between probabilistic inference in SPNs and tensor network contraction, opening avenues for cross-disciplinary optimization techniques.
proposals.novelty: While SPN structure learning has been parallelized, the novel insight is treating exact SPN *inference* as a distributed tensor contraction problem, applying HPC techniques from computational chemistry to machine learning in a way not explored in pre-2026 SPN literature.

proposals.title: Cluster-Parallel Inference for Large-Scale Sum-Product Networks via Structure-Aware Partitioning
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, deep probabilistic models, but their inference on massive datasets or high-treewidth models remains computationally prohibitive on single machines. As pre-2026 trends push towards larger models and data, the sequential nature of SPN sum and product operations becomes a critical bottleneck, limiting their application in real-time systems and large-scale probabilistic reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a structure-aware partitioning algorithm and a communication-efficient message-passing protocol, the exact inference computations of a large SPN can be distributed across a compute cluster, achieving near-linear speedup without approximation error for a broad class of network structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph partitioning algorithm that minimizes inter-cluster communication by analyzing the SPN's DAG structure, weighting edges by the expected message size. Inference will be parallelized using a Bulk Synchronous Parallel (BSP) model, where each cluster node performs local sum-product operations, followed by synchronized message passing across the network cut. We will implement this within the 'SPFlow' framework, optimizing for data locality and leveraging MPI for inter-node communication, with dynamic load balancing to handle skewed distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, cluster-parallel exact inference engine for SPNs, enabling their use on previously intractable problem scales. It will provide a systematic analysis of the parallelism inherent in probabilistic graphical model inference, contributing new benchmarks and scaling laws for distributed probabilistic learning.
proposals.novelty: While distributed deep learning is common, distributed *exact* inference for deep probabilistic models like SPNs is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the structure-aware decomposition that preserves exactness, moving beyond model parallelism for neural networks to a new formalism for probabilistic circuit parallelism.

proposals.title: Full-Distribution Inversion: Targeted Data Generation via Gradient Optimization on Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Current generative models, including deep generative networks, often lack the tractable likelihoods needed for precise, constrained data generation. Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer exact and efficient inference, making them powerful discriminative and density estimation models, but their generative use has been largely limited to ancestral sampling, which provides little control. Borrowing from geophysics, where Full Waveform Inversion uses a tractable forward model to solve an ill-posed inverse problem, we propose a novel generation paradigm that leverages the SPN's tractability for targeted synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: That the tractable likelihood and marginals of a learned SPN can serve as the forward model in an iterative inversion loop, enabling gradient-based optimization in the input space to generate data points that maximize probability under arbitrary, user-specified evidence constraints.
proposals.method: We will formalize the inverse generation task as an optimization problem: minimize a loss combining negative log-likelihood (computed efficiently via the SPN forward pass) and a constraint term (e.g., squared error on specified feature values). Using automatic differentiation through the SPN's computational graph—enabled by frameworks like PyTorch—we will perform gradient descent on a randomly initialized data vector to iteratively 'invert' the network toward high-probability regions. We will extend this core with variational formulations for latent variable conditioning and investigate second-order optimization methods for improved convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, precise method for constrained data generation from probabilistic models with tractable inference. It will expand the utility of SPNs beyond density estimation into controllable synthesis for data augmentation and counterfactual analysis. The geophysical analogy provides a principled optimization framework that could influence generative modeling in other tractable model families.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is treating an SPN not merely as a sampler but as a differentiable forward model for solving inverse problems, a concept directly inspired by seismic inversion techniques not previously applied to generative machine learning. This moves beyond standard sampling and Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods for SPNs.

proposals.title: Streaming Bayesian Sum-Product Networks for Online Generative Modeling and Data Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning systems increasingly operate on non-stationary data streams, where concept drift renders static models obsolete. While Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable inference and exact likelihood evaluation, their application has been largely limited to batch learning. An online, adaptive SPN that can continuously update its generative model from a data stream would enable real-time synthetic data generation for dynamic environments, a critical need in areas like robotics and reinforcement learning where data is scarce and expensive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating SPN learning as a process of incremental Bayesian parameter updating, we can maintain a generative model that adapts to concept drift in a data stream while retaining the tractability needed for efficient, exact sampling from the evolving posterior predictive distribution.
proposals.method: We will develop a streaming SPN framework where leaf distributions are conjugate-exponential models (e.g., Gaussians, categoricals) enabling closed-form Bayesian parameter updates upon observing new data. The network structure, learned initially via pre-2026 algorithms like LearnSPN, will be fixed, but we will derive and implement recursive Bayesian updates for all sum and product node parameters. This updated model will then be used for online ancestral sampling, generating synthetic data points that reflect the current estimated data distribution. We will integrate this into a reinforcement learning pipeline for dynamic data augmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first fully online, Bayesian SPN capable of adaptive generative modeling from data streams. It will provide a theoretically grounded method for real-time synthetic data generation that respects concept drift, directly applicable to streaming data augmentation and anomaly detection. The project will also demonstrate its utility in a reinforcement learning context, improving sample efficiency in non-stationary environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of SPNs' tractable exact inference with recursive Bayesian updating for all network parameters in an online setting, creating a closed-loop system for adaptive data synthesis, which is not addressed by prior batch SPN learning or approximate online deep generative models.

proposals.title: Efficient and Controllable Data Generation via Inverse Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models like GANs and VAEs are powerful but often lack the tractable inference and explicit probability semantics needed for controllable, interpretable generation. Sum-Product Networks (SPNs), as developed by Domingos and others, provide a tractable representation of complex distributions via efficient marginal and conditional inference. However, their primary use has been for discriminative tasks and density estimation, leaving their potential as structured, efficient generative samplers largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: The tractable, hierarchical probability distribution encoded by a learned SPN can be leveraged to perform efficient, high-quality sampling and controllable data generation by inverting its inference passes, outperforming standard deep generative models in terms of inference-time control and semantic editing.
proposals.method: We will develop algorithms for ancestral sampling from SPNs by exploiting their tree-structured, decomposable architecture. The core method involves a top-down pass where a root value is sampled and then conditionally propagated down the network, using the SPN's weights as conditional probabilities. We will extend this to conditional generation by clamping observed variable states and performing efficient marginal inference on the remaining sub-SPN. For high-dimensional data like images, we will integrate deep SPN architectures, potentially using convolutional and region-graph structures, and explore hybrid models where a deep neural network maps SPN-generated latent codes to the final output space.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish SPNs as a new class of tractable generative models, providing a formal framework for controllable sampling with guaranteed efficiency. It will contribute algorithms for conditional and intervened sampling, enabling applications in data augmentation, causal scenario generation, and structured reinforcement learning environments where model interpretability is critical.
proposals.novelty: While SPNs are known for tractable inference, systematically exploiting them for high-quality, controllable *generation* is a novel inverse problem. The approach is distinct from black-box deep generative models by offering exact likelihoods and closed-form conditioning within the sampling process itself.

proposals.title: Architectural Pruning for Robustness: Learning Compact Sum-Product Networks via Missing-Data-Aware Marginal Utility
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable inference, but their size can hinder deployment, and their performance degrades with missing inputs—a common scenario in real-world data. Existing pruning methods focus on size or accuracy on complete data, creating models fragile to missingness. A unified approach that learns compactness and robustness jointly is needed for reliable, efficient probabilistic models.
proposals.hypothesis: Pruning an SPN based on the marginal utility of its components during inference with missing data will yield a network that is simultaneously more compact and inherently more robust to missing inputs than one pruned for size or accuracy alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a pruning criterion that quantifies a component's (sum node, product node, leaf) contribution to inference accuracy across a Monte Carlo-sampled distribution of missing data patterns. This 'missing-data-aware utility' score will guide iterative, greedy pruning or a structured sparsity-inducing optimization (e.g., group Lasso) over network parameters and structure. The framework will integrate with existing SPN learners (LearnSPN, GD-based) to allow for pruning during or after learning, ensuring the final architecture is optimized for the dual objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for learning SPNs that are both small and robust, directly addressing a key deployment bottleneck. It will advance the theory of model compression for probabilistic graphical models by introducing a missing-data-centric objective. The resulting models will be more practical for applications like robotics and healthcare where data is often incomplete.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard pruning or robust training techniques, this method unifies architectural compression and robustness by defining utility directly in the context of missing-data inference, making robustness a core architectural feature rather than a learned parameterization.

proposals.title: Relevance-Guided Pruning and Structured Compression for Efficient Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable inference but can become large and computationally expensive after learning, especially when applied to high-dimensional data like images or for relational reasoning. This limits their deployment in resource-constrained environments like robotics or embedded systems. Post-training model compression is a critical but under-explored avenue for making SPNs more practical and scalable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of relevance-based node pruning, leveraging ideas from model stability and connectivity, and structured tensor decomposition of weight matrices can significantly reduce SPN size while preserving, or even improving, generalization performance.
proposals.method: We will first develop a pruning criterion based on the expected change in model output (relevance) upon node removal, inspired by Domingos's work on relevance-guided modeling. Second, we will apply low-rank tensor decomposition to the weight matrices of sum nodes, framing this as a constrained optimization problem to minimize reconstruction error. The methods will be evaluated on standard density estimation and relational datasets, measuring model size, inference speed, and log-likelihood.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for post-learning compression of SPNs, delivering smaller, faster models without costly retraining. It will extend the toolkit for efficient probabilistic inference and connect SPN research to broader model compression literature in deep learning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior SPN simplification work focused on learning structure, we target compression of already-trained networks. The integration of relevance metrics from reinforcement learning with tensor decomposition from deep learning optimization is a novel synthesis for this model class.

proposals.title: Stability-Plasticity Control in Sum-Product Networks for Online Learning Under Drift
proposals.motivation: Real-world data streams, such as those from robotic sensors or financial markets, are non-stationary, exhibiting concept drift that degrades the performance of static models. While online learning algorithms exist, they often face a fundamental stability-plasticity dilemma: adapting to new data can catastrophically forget previously learned concepts. This is particularly critical for structured probabilistic models like Sum-Product Networks (SPNs), which are valued for tractable inference but lack robust mechanisms for continual adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an SPN equipped with a lightweight, relevance-guided drift detector and a structured adaptation policy can achieve effective online learning by dynamically allocating model capacity, thereby balancing stability and plasticity without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-component architecture. First, a statistical drift detector will monitor the sufficient statistics of the SPN's leaf distributions to signal significant shifts. Upon detection, a structured adaptation phase will be triggered, guided by principles from Domingos's 'Relevance-Guided Modeling' work. This phase will employ strategies such as (1) adding new sum nodes with children specialized for the new data regime, or (2) softly freezing a subset of stable parameters while re-optimizing others, using constrained gradient-based updates to minimize interference with previously learned stable concepts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel online learning algorithm for SPNs that explicitly manages concept drift, extending their applicability to lifelong learning scenarios. It will provide a formal framework for stability-plasticity control in tractable probabilistic networks, contributing a new benchmark for continual learning that emphasizes both adaptation and knowledge retention.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in integrating explicit drift detection with structured, capacity-allocating adaptation mechanisms within the SPN framework, moving beyond simple online gradient descent or ensemble methods to address forgetting through model architecture intervention.

proposals.title: Event-Driven Online Learning in Sum-Product Networks via Synaptic Plasticity Rules
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning struggles with non-stationary, high-volume streaming data, as standard batch and online gradient descent methods often require extensive retraining or suffer from catastrophic forgetting. Sum-Product Networks (SPNs), while tractable and expressive, are primarily trained offline, limiting their applicability to real-time, lifelong learning scenarios in robotics and sensor networks. Drawing a direct analogy to biological neural systems, which adapt continuously via local synaptic rules like Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP), offers a promising but underexplored path to scalable, stable online learning.
proposals.hypothesis: That SPNs can be adapted for effective, stable online learning on non-stationary streams by implementing parameter update rules inspired by synaptic plasticity, where local, event-driven updates based on prediction error ('surprise') can balance plasticity and stability without full retraining.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel online EM-inspired algorithm for SPNs where each sum node weight is updated incrementally. Upon receiving a new data point, the algorithm computes the posterior responsibility of each child node (a local signal). The weight update will be modulated by a surprise signal, defined as the Kullback-Leibler divergence between prior and posterior responsibilities, mimicking the role of neuromodulators in STDP. This results in a local, Hebbian-like rule: Δw_ij ∝ surprise * (posterior responsibility_i - w_ij). We will integrate this with a node-specific memory decay factor to mitigate forgetting, implementing a form of synaptic consolidation.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new online learning algorithm for SPNs that enables lifelong adaptation to data streams, directly addressing the SCALING_DUAL challenge. It will provide a principled, neuroscience-inspired framework for balancing stability and plasticity in probabilistic graphical models. The resulting method will be empirically validated on robotic manipulation and video activity recognition streaming datasets.
proposals.novelty: While online EM variants exist, the explicit formulation of a local, surprise-modulated plasticity rule for SPNs, drawing a formal analogy to computational models of STDP, is novel. This moves beyond standard gradient-based online learning by embedding neuro-inspired stability mechanisms directly into the SPN's parameter update scheme.

proposals.title: Online, Anytime Structure and Parameter Learning for Streaming Visual Data with Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Current deep vision systems are predominantly trained offline on static datasets, making them brittle and inefficient for lifelong robotic agents or systems processing continuous video streams. While Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable probabilistic modeling, their application to vision has been limited to batch learning settings. Bridging this gap is crucial for creating adaptive, efficient, and explainable perception systems that operate in non-stationary real-world environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By developing novel online gradient-based and Bayesian moment-matching algorithms for SPN parameters, coupled with data-driven, incremental structure learning heuristics, we can create a vision-capable SPN that learns continually from a data stream without catastrophic forgetting, maintaining both high accuracy and tractable inference.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid convolutional and region-based SPN architecture for visual input. For online parameter learning, we will derive stochastic natural gradient updates and Bayesian online moment matching for leaf distributions. For structure adaptation, we will employ streaming clustering on learned feature representations to trigger localized node splitting, guided by statistical independence tests (e.g., online G-tests) to identify new feature combinations. A replay buffer of compressed 'pseudo-data' summaries from the SPN's internal distributions will mitigate forgetting.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield the first framework for continual, online structure and parameter learning in deep SPNs for vision. It will provide new algorithms for streaming probabilistic model adaptation and a benchmark for continual visual learning that evaluates both accuracy and inference-time tractability, advancing toward more autonomous and adaptive machine perception.
proposals.novelty: The integration of *online structure learning* mechanisms into deep, vision-oriented SPNs is unexplored. Prior SPN online learning work focuses on shallow models or fixed structures; we propose a holistic, anytime adaptation of both parameters and architecture for high-dimensional, non-i.i.d. visual streams.

proposals.title: Online Bayesian Moment Matching for Streaming Data with Sum-Product Networks
proposals.motivation: Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) offer tractable, deep probabilistic models for complex data, but their standard batch learning algorithms are ill-suited for real-world streaming applications where data arrives continuously. Pre-2026, online learning for deep probabilistic models, especially SPNs, remains under-explored, limiting their use in robotics and reinforcement learning systems that must adapt incrementally. This gap hinders the deployment of SPNs in dynamic environments where models must update without costly retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SPN parameters can be adapted online via a principled Bayesian moment matching framework, yielding a stable, efficient, and consistent learner for non-stationary data streams. This will preserve the representational power of SPNs while enabling lifelong learning.
proposals.method: We will develop an online variational algorithm based on assumed density filtering. Upon receiving a new data mini-batch, we will perform a single forward-backward pass to compute sufficient statistics, then update the SPN's leaf and weight distributions via Bayesian moment matching to minimize KL divergence to the updated posterior. We will incorporate a change-point detection mechanism, inspired by relevance-guided modeling, to trigger selective network structure adjustments (e.g., splitting sum nodes) when data distribution shifts significantly. Optimization will use natural gradients for efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive online learning algorithm for SPNs capable of handling concept drift, directly enabling their use in streaming robotics and reinforcement learning scenarios. It will contribute a theoretical analysis of the online SPN's convergence properties and an empirical demonstration on benchmarks and a robotic manipulation task with streaming visual data.
proposals.novelty: While online learning exists for simple models, applying a Bayesian moment matching paradigm to incrementally adapt the parameters and structure of a deep, tractable probabilistic circuit like an SPN is novel. The integration of stability-driven structural adaptation from Domingos's prior work into an online SPN context is a unique synthesis.

ideas.title: Distribute SPN inference across clusters
ideas.description: Parallelize the sum and product operations for large-scale models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sum-product networks: A new deep architecture

ideas.title: Missing-Data-Aware Pruning for Robust and Compact Sum-Product Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework for learning compact and robust Sum-Product Networks (SPNs). Instead of pruning for size or accuracy alone on complete data, the core innovation is to prune or compress the network based on a component's marginal utility during inference with missing inputs. The method will identify and retain the network structures (sum nodes, product nodes, leaf distributions) that are most critical for maintaining performance across a distribution of possible missing data patterns, while aggressively removing parts that are redundant or fragile to missingness. This results in an SPN that is inherently smaller and natively more robust to missing data, as its very architecture is optimized for this dual objective from the ground up.

ideas.title: Apply belief propagation to SPN learning
ideas.description: Use message-passing algorithms from graphical models for parameter learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sum-product networks: A new deep architecture

ideas.title: Boosting-Constructed Sum-Product Networks for Efficient and Robust Large-Scale Vision
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework for large-scale computer vision by constructing Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) using boosting algorithms. Instead of designing a fixed, monolithic SPN architecture, the network is built incrementally, where each boosting round adds a new layer or sub-network that focuses on correcting the errors or uncertainties of the current ensemble. This results in a deep, probabilistically sound model that is both data-efficient and highly scalable. The approach uniquely combines the tractable inference of SPNs with the adaptive capacity and robustness of boosting, making it particularly suited for complex vision tasks like scene understanding and fine-grained recognition where model calibration and handling of hard examples are critical.

ideas.title: Hybrid SPNs with Variational Embeddings for Intractable Modules
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that all components of a Sum-Product Network (SPN) must be tractable. Inspired by hybrid methods in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) like RANS-LES coupling, we propose a hybrid SPN architecture. The network is decomposed into domains: a main tractable SPN backbone handles regions where exact inference is crucial, while specific, potentially more expressive sub-networks with intractable partition functions (e.g., certain deep neural modules) are embedded as specialized modules. The key innovation is adapting the CFD concept of a solution-adaptive interface. Instead of fixed boundary conditions, we design a variational embedding layer that acts as an interface between tractable and intractable modules. This interface uses a learned approximate posterior to pass sufficient statistics and error gradients, allowing the intractable modules to be trained via approximate methods (e.g., variational inference, Monte Carlo) while being seamlessly integrated into and regularized by the globally tractable SPN framework. This expands the representational power of SPNs without sacrificing overall structural learning and inference guarantees where they are most needed.

ideas.title: Benchmarking SPNs: A Systematic Framework for Architectural Comparison
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to address the need for empirical and theoretical comparison of Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) with other deep learning architectures by drawing an analogy from computational chemistry's approach to comparing molecular force fields. In that field, new models are evaluated using standardized benchmark suites with diverse, specific tasks and controlled protocols to ensure fair, multi-dimensional comparisons. Adapting this solution, the research will develop a systematic benchmarking framework for deep probabilistic architectures. This framework will define a canonical suite of probabilistic reasoning tasks—such as density estimation, marginal inference, and conditional query answering—using standardized datasets, evaluation metrics, and fixed computational constraints. The goal is to move beyond isolated comparisons and provide a rigorous, reproducible, and holistic analysis of where SPNs excel or fall short relative to architectures like Deep Belief Networks, Variational Autoencoders, and standard deep neural networks, thereby clarifying their theoretical and practical niche.

ideas.title: Applying SPNs to Large-Scale Vision via Hierarchical Bayesian Network Scalability Principles
ideas.description: The original idea proposes applying Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) to large-scale computer vision tasks, a challenge due to the typical scalability limits of monolithic probabilistic models. Drawing an analogy from computational ecology, where hierarchical Bayesian networks successfully model continent-scale species distributions by decomposing them into local, linked sub-models, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to develop a hierarchical SPN architecture for vision. This framework would decompose a large-scale vision problem (e.g., ImageNet classification or scene understanding) into a collection of smaller, domain-specific SPNs, coordinated through a shared parameter hierarchy and prior structure. This approach aims to make SPNs scalable and practical for billion-pixel datasets by enabling parallel training, incorporating heterogeneous visual features, and maintaining tractable exact inference, thus bridging the gap between deep learning scale and probabilistic graphical model rigor.

ideas.title: Develop Structure and Parameter Learning for SPNs via Expectation-Maximization and Greedy Search, Inspired by Phylogenetic Tree Construction
ideas.description: The original idea identifies the need for learning algorithms for Sum-Product Networks (SPNs). Drawing an analogy from computational biology, specifically phylogenetic tree construction, which solves the analogous problem of learning a hierarchical probabilistic model (a tree) from sequence data. The field's solution combines Expectation-Maximization for parameter (branch length) optimization with heuristic search strategies for structure (topology) learning. Adapted to SPNs, this suggests a research direction to develop a learning algorithm that iteratively performs: 1) Expectation-Maximization to optimize the weights of sum nodes and the parameters of leaf distributions, and 2) a greedy search through the space of network structures, perhaps by recursively partitioning variables and data based on likelihood gains, to discover an effective hierarchical decomposition. This integrated approach aims to automate the construction of both the architecture and the parameters of an SPN.

ideas.title: Direct Continuous-Distribution SPNs for Controllable, High-Fidelity Data Generation
ideas.description: This research direction develops Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) with native, tractable continuous leaf distributions (e.g., Gaussians, mixtures of exponentials) to model raw, high-dimensional continuous data directly. The tractable inference properties of these continuous SPNs are then leveraged to design exact and efficient ancestral sampling algorithms that operate directly on the continuous representation. This synergy enables the generation of high-fidelity, realistic synthetic samples—such as audio waveforms, sensor readings, or financial time-series—without the information loss or artifacts introduced by discretization. The result is a single, end-to-end probabilistic deep generative model that supports both exact density estimation and high-quality data generation from continuous domains.

ideas.title: Continual Online Learning for Streaming Visual Worlds with Sum-Product Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that fuses the architectural strengths of SPNs for tractable probabilistic reasoning in vision with online, incremental learning algorithms. The core innovation is a system capable of processing continuous, high-dimensional visual data streams—such as live video feeds or lifelong robot perception—and adapting its internal SPN parameters and structure in real-time. This allows the model to continually integrate new visual concepts, refine its understanding of existing ones, and model evolving scene distributions, all while maintaining computational efficiency and avoiding performance collapse on previously learned tasks. The result is a step toward vision systems that learn perpetually and autonomously from the dynamic visual world.

ideas.title: Continuous SPNs via Kernel Density Leaves and Mixture Models
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the common assumption in Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) that continuous variables must be discretized for tractability. Drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics, specifically Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH), which models continuous fields using particle-based kernel smoothing without a fixed grid. Adapting this solution, we propose to develop SPN formulations where leaf nodes are continuous probability density estimators (like kernel density estimates or Gaussian mixtures) instead of discrete distributions. This approach allows the SPN to model continuous variables directly, preserving information and gradient signals, enabling more accurate and efficient learning and inference on continuous data without the artifacts and information loss introduced by discretization.

ideas.title: Develop learning algorithms for SPNs
ideas.description: The paper states that learning algorithms for SPNs need to be developed.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sum-product networks: A new deep architecture

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust and Compact SPNs for Secure Embedded Inference
ideas.description: This research develops miniaturized Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) specifically designed to be robust against adversarial attacks for secure, low-power embedded inference. The approach jointly optimizes for model size, energy efficiency, and adversarial robustness by integrating tailored adversarial training procedures within the SPN learning algorithm. The resulting models are equipped with lightweight, runtime monitors that detect distributional shifts indicative of adversarial inputs. This creates a secure-by-design embedded intelligence system suitable for applications where data integrity and reliable operation are critical.

ideas.title: Applying Evolutionary Strategy Gradient Estimation to SPN Training
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting a solution from evolutionary robotics and policy search to train Sum-Product Networks (SPNs). The analogous problem is training complex policies in non-differentiable or black-box environments, solved by algorithms like Natural Evolution Strategies (NES), which estimate gradients through parameter sampling rather than backpropagation. The adapted research idea applies this gradient estimation principle to SPNs: by treating SPN parameters as a distribution, sampling perturbations, and estimating the gradient of the log-likelihood objective, we can perform robust, derivative-free optimization. This bypasses potential difficulties in deriving and implementing exact backpropagation through the SPN's probabilistic graph, offering an alternative training paradigm inspired by evolutionary optimization techniques.

ideas.title: Infer structure instead of assuming it
ideas.description: Learn the network structure automatically from data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sum-product networks: A new deep architecture

ideas.title: Apply Error-Correcting Code Decoding to SPN Structure Learning
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from digital communications. The analogous problem is error-correction in low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, solved using the sum-product algorithm (a belief propagation method) on a factor graph to iteratively pass messages and infer correct bits. We adapt this solution to SPN learning by framing the search for an optimal SPN structure as a decoding problem on a graph of potential components. Messages passed between nodes will encode constraints and likelihoods for region decompositions and variable subsets, enabling a unified, message-passing-based algorithm to jointly infer both the hierarchical structure (graph topology) and the parameters of the SPN, thereby enhancing both learning efficiency and model accuracy.

ideas.title: Compare SPNs to other deep models
ideas.description: Empirical and theoretical comparison with other deep learning architectures is needed.

ideas.title: Inverse SPN Generation via Geophysical Seismic Inversion Techniques
ideas.description: This idea explores the inverse problem of generating data from Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic inversion. In seismology, the inverse problem of inferring subsurface structure from surface measurements is solved using the tractable forward model of wave propagation in an iterative optimization loop (e.g., Full Waveform Inversion). Adapted to SPNs, we propose using the network's own tractable distribution—its ability to efficiently compute likelihoods and marginal probabilities—as the forward model within a similar iterative generation framework. Instead of merely sampling from the learned distribution, the system would perform gradient-based or variational optimization on a data point in the input space, using the SPN's computations to guide the point toward regions of high probability under desired constraints (e.g., specific feature values or latent interpretations), enabling efficient, targeted, and high-fidelity data generation.

ideas.title: Online, Adaptive Data Synthesis via Streaming SPN Generation
ideas.description: This research develops a method for online, generative modeling with Sum-Product Networks (SPNs). The core innovation is to perform incremental Bayesian parameter updates on the SPN as new streaming data arrives, ensuring the model adapts to concept drift. Simultaneously, the tractable likelihood and partition function of the continually updated SPN are leveraged to perform efficient and exact sampling from the current posterior predictive distribution. This closed-loop system enables real-time generation of synthetic data that is statistically consistent with the most recent observations, providing a powerful tool for streaming data augmentation, anomaly detection, and dynamic simulation.

ideas.title: Study the inverse problem: generating from SPNs

ideas.title: Dynamically Constrained Inference via Amortized Meta-Reasoning
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the intractability of relaxed structural constraints by integrating a learned meta-reasoning module that dynamically manages the inference process. The meta-reasoner is trained to predict where completeness or decomposability can be safely approximated and where exact constraints must be enforced to preserve accuracy. This enables efficient, adaptive inference that maintains strong theoretical guarantees where needed while allowing controlled approximations elsewhere. The system thus provides a robust, scalable framework for complex models where full structural constraints are prohibitive.

ideas.title: Incorporate Elastic Net and Group Lasso Regularization from Genomics into SPN Structure Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts regularization techniques from high-dimensional genomics to prevent overfitting in Sum-Product Networks (SPNs). In genomics, Elastic Net and Group Lasso are used for structured feature selection when predicting traits from thousands of correlated genes, applying penalties at both the individual and group level. The adapted solution applies this principle to SPNs by imposing Group Lasso-style regularization on the weights of sum nodes, treating the set of weights for a sum node's children as a group. This encourages the model to prune entire branches or select simpler mixture components, thereby learning a sparser, more robust network structure. Combining this with traditional L2 regularization on leaf parameters creates a hybrid 'SPN Elastic Net' that controls both parameter magnitude and graph complexity, directly addressing overfitting through structured sparsity induction.

ideas.title: Distributed SPN Inference via MapReduce-Style Tensor Contraction
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational chemistry, specifically the parallelization of high-dimensional tensor contractions in electronic structure calculations. In that field, massive tensor networks are evaluated by partitioning them and distributing independent contraction operations across an HPC cluster using a MapReduce-style paradigm, followed by a hierarchical reduction of partial results. Adapted to SPN inference, the SPN's computational graph is viewed as a tensor network. The inference process is decomposed into a sequence of distributed sum and product operations. The SPN structure and input data are partitioned, and independent sub-network evaluations are mapped to different cluster nodes. A coordinated reduction phase then aggregates these partial results through a communication-efficient tree, enabling large-scale SPN inference on distributed clusters without requiring the entire model to fit in a single machine's memory.

ideas.title: Allow non-tractable sub-networks within SPNs
ideas.description: Incorporate modules with intractable partition functions using approximate methods.

ideas.title: Identifying Phase-Transition-Like Boundaries for Tractable Partition Functions via Complexity Classes
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to explore the general conditions for tractable partition functions by drawing an analogy from statistical physics. In that field, the behavior of complex systems is understood by identifying phase transitions—sharp boundaries in a control parameter space that separate regimes with qualitatively different, often analytically tractable, macroscopic properties. The analogous solution involves mapping these boundaries using order parameters and specialized approximation methods. Adapted to the context of partition functions in computer science and statistical inference, the research would seek to treat computational tractability itself as a 'phase.' The goal is to define relevant complexity-theoretic order parameters (e.g., based on interaction strength, graph topology, or presence of symmetry) and rigorously characterize the boundaries in the space of model parameters where the partition function transitions from being computable or approximable in polynomial time to becoming #P-hard. This would provide a principled, predictive framework for identifying broad subclasses of graphical models with guaranteed tractable inference, analogous to how phase diagrams guide physicists.

ideas.title: Approximate Inference via Loosely-Coupled Subsystems
ideas.description: This idea explores inference under relaxed structural constraints by drawing an analogy from Control Systems Engineering. In that field, large-scale systems are managed not through perfect, centralized models but via decentralized architectures with overlapping decompositions and intermittent coordination, ensuring stability despite approximate coupling. Adapting this solution, we propose to develop probabilistic inference frameworks for 'loosely-coupled' graphical models. The research would define metrics for inter-cluster coupling strength and create approximate message-passing or variational algorithms that perform updates selectively—only when local belief changes are significant relative to the coupling. This relaxes the strict completeness and decomposability constraints of exact inference, trading off a provably bounded approximation error for substantial gains in computational scalability, particularly in large, weakly-interacting systems.

ideas.title: Relax the completeness and decomposability constraints

ideas.title: Foundational Embedding and Complexity Translation for SPNs via Arithmetic Circuit Isomorphisms
ideas.description: This research formally establishes a bridge between SPNs and arithmetic circuit complexity by constructing explicit, structure-preserving mappings from classes of SPNs to specific types of arithmetic circuits (e.g., monotone, multilinear). It then performs a targeted analysis to determine which arithmetic complexity results—such as lower bounds on circuit size for computing certain polynomial families—translate into meaningful statements about the representational capacity and minimal network structure required for SPNs to model target probability distributions. The outcome is a principled complexity theory for SPNs that moves beyond analogy to provide concrete, provable limitations and guarantees on their efficiency.

ideas.title: Online Learning for Streaming Data via Synaptic Plasticity Rules
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea of creating online learning for streaming data by adapting SPN parameters incrementally. It draws an analogy from computational neuroscience, where the problem of continuous learning from an endless stream of input is solved by biological synapses through local plasticity rules like STDP. The analogous solution involves incremental, event-driven weight updates that balance stability and plasticity. Adapted to this context, the idea is to design a novel online learning algorithm for SPNs inspired by these rules. Each node in the SPN would have its parameters updated based on local computations triggered by incoming data, using a mechanism that incorporates a 'surprise' or prediction error signal to determine the magnitude and direction of updates. This approach aims to enable SPNs to scale effectively for non-stationary streaming data, maintaining accuracy over time without requiring retraining on the entire historical dataset, thus addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension of managing both data volume and model complexity.

ideas.title: Federated, Swarm-like Inference via Miniaturized SPN Clusters
ideas.description: This research fuses miniaturization and distribution to create a system where numerous ultra-low-power embedded devices, each hosting a tiny, specialized SPN fragment, collaboratively perform inference for a large-scale probabilistic model. The key innovation is a decentralized protocol that allows these devices to dynamically form ad-hoc clusters, parallelize the sum-product algorithm across the network, and pass partial computations peer-to-peer to converge on a global inference result. This enables complex probabilistic reasoning in environments like sensor networks or robotic swarms, where central compute is unavailable, transforming a collection of simple nodes into a cohesive, intelligent computational fabric.

ideas.title: Drift-Aware Online Learning for Streaming Data with Stability-Plasticity Control
ideas.description: This system performs online learning for streaming data by adaptively updating Sum-Product Network (SPN) parameters. Its core innovation is a dual-component architecture: a lightweight drift detector continuously monitors the data stream for significant distributional shifts. When drift is confirmed, the system enters a structured adaptation phase, employing strategies like network expansion or parameter isolation to integrate new knowledge without catastrophically overwriting stable, previously learned concepts. This approach maintains a reliable model over time by explicitly balancing the plasticity needed for new data with the stability required to preserve past learning.

ideas.title: Impute Missing Inference Inputs via Bayesian Data Augmentation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that models require complete, observed inputs at inference time. Drawing an analogy from Ecology, where species distribution models handle missing occurrence data via Bayesian data augmentation, we propose a native inference-time solution for missing features. The adapted method involves a hierarchical model with a core predictive process and an observation model for missingness. During inference for a partially observed sample, the model treats missing features as latent variables, using Bayesian (or variational) inference to jointly sample their plausible values conditioned on the observed features and the prediction task. This generates not just a single prediction but a predictive distribution that natively accounts for the uncertainty introduced by the missing data, enabling robust inference without requiring separate, pre-processing imputation steps that ignore this uncertainty.

ideas.title: Applying Phylogenetic Tree Analysis Tools to SPN Circuit Complexity
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from computational biology, specifically phylogenetics, where tools analyze the complexity and relationships of evolutionary trees. Phylogenetic methods score trees based on the minimal number of evolutionary operations (like mutations) required to explain observed data, using concepts like parsimony. We adapt this to the analysis of Sum-Product Networks (SPNs) by viewing their directed acyclic graph structure as a computational 'phylogeny'. The adapted research idea is to develop SPN complexity measures inspired by phylogenetic scoring. Specifically, we define a 'computational parsimony score' for an SPN that counts the minimal essential sum and product operations (or unique node functions) required to represent its computed distribution. This provides a new theoretical tool from an analogous field to establish lower bounds, compare the expressive efficiency of different SPN architectures, and analyze the trade-offs between depth, width, and representational power, similar to how biologists compare the complexity of evolutionary hypotheses.

ideas.title: Inferring Network Topology via Crystallographic Diffraction Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a network's connectivity must be known or predefined. Drawing an analogy from X-ray crystallography, where a crystal's atomic structure is inferred from its diffraction pattern, we propose to infer network structure from the 'pattern' of its dynamical outputs. The analogous solution uses iterative phase retrieval algorithms to solve the inverse problem of reconstructing a lattice from intensity data. Adapted to network science, this means developing computational methods that take time-series or relational outcome data as input, treat the network adjacency matrix as the unknown 'crystal,' and use optimization techniques that incorporate constraints (like sparsity or degree distributions) to automatically learn the underlying graph structure that best explains the observed dynamical patterns, without assuming a prior architectural template.

ideas.title: Automated Complexity-Theoretic Delineation of the Partition Function Tractability Frontier
ideas.description: This research program aims to systematically map the boundary between tractable and intractable classes of partition functions using a formal, automated framework. It defines a parameterized complexity space for partition function problems based on structural decompositions of their interaction hypergraphs and algebraic properties of their local functions. The core of the approach uses interactive theorem provers and algorithm synthesis tools to automatically generate and verify proofs of polynomial-time computability or #P-hardness for families of functions defined by these parameters. The outcome will be a dynamically generated, rigorously proven 'complexity phase diagram' that predicts the computational cost of a partition function from its specification, moving beyond ad-hoc sufficient conditions.

ideas.title: Robust Structure Discovery with Constrained Hypothesis Testing
ideas.description: This approach learns network structure from data but does so within a constrained, multi-hypothesis testing framework to avoid spurious discovery. It uses adaptive regularization and environment-based validation to distinguish stable, generalizable relationships from noise or dataset-specific artifacts. The system outputs not just a single graph, but a set of plausible structures ranked by their robustness and simplicity, enabling more reliable inference and clearer interpretation.

ideas.title: Cellular Organelle-Inspired Subnetworks for Embedded SPNs
ideas.description: This research adapts the biological solution of cellular compartmentalization into organelles to the challenge of creating miniaturized, efficient SPNs for embedded systems. In cell biology, complex processes are made efficient by segregating them into specialized, optimized organelles within a confined cellular volume. The adapted idea is to design a modular SPN architecture consisting of multiple, very small, specialized 'subnet organelle' computational modules—each a minimal SPN hard-optimized for a distinct, frequent inference operation (e.g., a specific marginal probability calculation). A lightweight, power-aware scheduler would dynamically activate only the necessary module(s) for a given query, keeping the vast majority of the network dormant. This approach minimizes the active silicon area and memory footprint at any moment, directly translating to the low-power, high-efficiency inference required for embedded and IoT devices, effectively solving the scaling dual challenge of maintaining functionality while drastically reducing resource use.
